content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 19, 1993


Jane Moore
Coordinator
Community Services/Program Development
Riverside County Office of Education
P.O. Box 868
Riverside, CA  92502-0868

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-93-307

Dear Ms. Moore:

     You have requested a confirmation of advice provided to you
on behalf of the Riverside County Office of Education on August
10, 1993, regarding the mass mailing prohibition of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1  In our telephone conversation of
August 11, 1993, you confirmed that you were requesting this
advice on behalf of Dr. Dale S. Holmes, the elected Riverside
Superintendent of Schools.

                                   QUESTION

     Under what circumstances can a report of the Riverside County
Commission on the Future of Education be distributed if included
with the report is a letter written and signed by the elected
Riverside Superintendent of Schools?

                                   CONCLUSION

     Only 200 copies of the report with the cover letter may be
circulated with public funds.  However, copies that are (1) picked
up by members of the public at your offices or schools, (2) copies
that are distributed in response to unsolicited specific requests
for the material or passed out at meetings, and (3) copies
distributed to other governmental agencies and offices in the
regular course of business are exempt and are not counted toward
the 201 that constitute a mass mailing.


_________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-307
Page 2


FACTS

The Riverside County Office of Education is preparing to
distribute a report issued by the Riverside County Commission on
the Future of Education (the "county commission"). The county
commission is made up of appointed community volunteers, and was
created to prepare The 1993 Riverside County Commission on the
Future of Education Report. The development and distribution of
the report will be paid for with public funds. Hewlett Packard is
printing copies of the report for the Riverside County Office of
Education.

You stated that you anticipate that copies of the report will
be distributed to the 27 school district superintendents in the
county and their boards. In addition, you intend to send 100
copies to community leaders and legislators. Remaining copies
will be distributed at meetings and made available at the offices
of the Riverside County Office of Education.

ANALYSIS

The Mass Mailings Prohibition

In June 1988, Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act
to provide: "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." Regulation 18901(a)² clarifies that a mailing is
prohibited only if all of the following apply:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box. For
purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
written document.

(2) The item sent either:

(A) Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, or

(B) Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, consultation,

2 The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in
Watson v. Fair Political Practices Com. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d
1059.

Our File No. A-93-307
Page 3


                    coordination, or concert with the elected
               officer;

                    (3)(A)  Any of the costs of distribution is
               paid for with public moneys; or

                    (B)  Costs of design, production, and
               printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
               moneys, and the design, production, or
               printing is done with the intent of sending
               the item other than as permitted by this
               regulation.

                    (4)  More than two hundred substantially
               similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
               excluding any item sent in response to an
               unsolicited request and any item described in
               subdivision (b);

                                        Regulation 18901(a).

     Thus, Regulation 18901(a) only restricts items that:

     1.  Have been mailed or delivered, by any means, to a
person's home, office or post office box. If items are set out
for the public to pick up on their own, or are handed out in a
public area, the restrictions of the regulation do not apply.3

     2.  Include the name, photograph or any reference to an
elected officer who is affiliated with the agency that produces or
distributes the mailing.4 (Regulation 18901(a)(2).)

     Additionally, if the mailing has been prepared or sent in
cooperation, consultation, coordination or concert with the
elected officer, any use of the elected officer's name, photograph

_______________________
     3 Please note however, that where an item falls within the
restrictions of Regulation 18901, you should be cautious that the
item is not redistributed by one of the prohibited means. We
would suggest a cover letter be included with any bulk delivery,
to make clear that the items are not to be redistributed to a
person's home, places of employment or post office box.
(Lavagetto Advice Letter No. A-90-199.)

     4 An elected officer is "affiliated" with an agency if the
officer is a member, officer or employee of the agency or a
subunit (such as a committee) of that agency; has supervisory
control over the agency; or appoints any of the members of the
agency. (Regulation 18901(c)(1); Riddle Advice Letter, No.
A-89-096.) Newsletters are not restricted with respect to
references to nonaffiliated elected officers or to persons who are
not elected officers.

Our File No. A-93-307
Page 4


or office, or any reference to the officer is prohibited.5
(Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B).)

     3.  The mailing is sent at public expense. Regulation
18901(a)(3) specifies that a mass mailing is "sent at public
expense" within the meaning of Section 89001 if either the costs
of distribution are paid for with public moneys6 or more than
$50.00 in public money is paid for the costs of design or
production, and the design or printing is done with the intent of
sending the item.

     4.  The mailing is a "mass mailing" as defined in the Act.
"Mass mailing" means over two hundred substantially similar pieces
of mail, but does not include mail which is sent in response to an
unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry, or any items that
fall within the exceptions set forth in Regulation 18901(b).
(Section 82041.5; Regulation 18901(a)(4).) If less than 201 of
the same or substantially similar items are sent in a calendar
month, the regulation would not restrict the mailing.

     According to your facts, the mailing was produced with public
funds, and will be distributed to more that 200 persons.
Additionally, you wish to include a personalized letter from
Dr. Holmes, an elected officer of the county.  Thus, absent a
specific exception, the mass mailing prohibition would apply.

Exceptions

     As stated above, Regulation 18901 provides exemptions whereby
an otherwise prohibited newsletter or mass mailing may be
distributed.


_________________________
     5  If the mailing has not been prepared or sent in cooperation,
consultation, coordination or concert with the elected officer,
use of the elected officer's name is permitted provided the
officer is not featured in the mailing. (Regulation
18901(a)(2)(A).) An elected officer is "featured" in a mass
mailing if he or she is singled out for the attention of the
reader by use of his or her signature, inclusion in any
photograph, or the manner of display of his or her name or office
in the layout of the document such as by headlines, type size, or
typeface. (Regulation 18901(c)(2).)

     6  By reference to Penal Code Section 426, Section 85102(e)
defines "public moneys" to include all bonds and evidence of
indebtedness, and all moneys belonging to the state, or any city,
county, town, district, or public agency therein, and all moneys,
bonds, and evidence of indebtedness received or held by state,
county, district, city, town, or public agency officers in their
official capacity.

Our File No. A-93-307
Page 5


     1.  Regulation 18901(c)(4) provides that mailings that
contain only information or material sent in response to an
unsolicited specific request are not counted toward the 201 that
equal to a mass mailing.  As used in the regulation, a request is
unsolicited if it is not requested or induced by the elected
officer or any other person acting at his or her request.
(Regulation 18901(c)(4)(A); Alquist Advice Letter, No. I-89-109.)
This would apply to copies picked up at your offices or passed out
at meetings, and would include copies sent to persons who make an
unsolicited specific request for the material.

     2.  Regulation 18901 also exempts intergovernmental and
intra-governmental mailings sent in the normal course of business.
(Regulation 18901(b)(3) and 18901 (b)(4).)  Mailings to school
teachers and administrators fall within this exemption so long as
they are sent in the normal course of business.7  (Mott-Smith
Advice Letter, No. I-89-108; Waggoner Advice Letter, No. A-92-
247.)

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.8

                                          Sincerely,

                                          Wayne Ordos
                                          Executive Director

                                          By: John W. Wallace
                                              Counsel, Legal Division




7     Please note that the exception for inter-agency mailings is
limited to mailings to the governmental entity or officer.
Materials provided to the schools for distribution to the students
would not qualify under this exception. (See, Alquist Advice
Letter, supra.)

8     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under what circumstances can a report of the Riverside County
Commission on the Future of Education be distributed if included
with the report is a letter written and signed by the elected
Riverside Superintendent of Schools?

CONCLUSION: Only 200 copies of the report with the cover letter may be
circulated with public funds.
However, copies that are
(1)
picked
up by members of the public at your offices or schools,
(2)
copies
that are distributed in response to unsolicited specific requests
for the material or passed out at meetings,
and
(3)
copies
distributed to other governmental agencies and officers in the
regular course of business are exempt and are not counted toward
the 201 that constitute a mass mailing.
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93--307
